revised_position: WebSocket remains the right choice for notification systems that must work reliably across adverse network conditions — mobile carriers, public WiFi, captive portals, 2G/3G transitions — but I've moderated significantly. Elena is correct that SSE is the right default for applications running on controlled infrastructure: corporate internal tools, CDN-backed dashboards, same-datacenter services where the network path is known and stable. My bias came from watching WebSocket implementations that invested in reconnection, backoff, and health monitoring succeed where naive SSE implementations failed — but that's not a fair comparison. SSE with a well-designed fallback transport (or wrapping EventSource in a solid reconnection layer) beats WebSocket for the 80% case. The remaining 20% — sub-100ms delivery requirements, bidirectional flows, binary payloads, and mobile-first audiences — WebSocket still wins, and the reconnection tooling gap Elena points to is closing (standardized patterns, libraries like reconnecting-websocket, HTTP/2 WebSocket support improving). My original position had the right enemy (infrastructure that kills TCP connections hates both transports equally) but the wrong conclusion.

conceded_to.point: The HTTP intermediary story is real. WebSocket upgrade configuration at every hop — ALBs, CDNs, reverse proxies, corporate forward proxies — is a genuine operational tax that SSE avoids entirely, and silent misconfiguration at any layer is a nightmare to debug.

SSE's error visibility via HTTP status codes is legitimately better than WebSocket close frame hunting. When an SSE connection fails, you get a standard code. When a WebSocket dies behind a proxy that blocks upgrades, you often get nothing actionable.

The reconnect storm risk from poorly implemented custom WebSocket reconnection is a real production concern at scale. SSE's built-in EventSource reconnection, even with its quirks, is more predictable than the median team's homegrown exponential backoff.

disagrees_with.point: The 'SSE rides on HTTP so intermediaries handle it' argument is surface-level. In practice, mobile carriers and corporate proxies kill long-lived HTTP connections too — carrier NAT timeouts of 30 seconds, captive portals, network transitions. SSE connections die from the same infrastructure failures. The difference isn't that one transport survives better — it's that WebSocket's reconnection patterns are more battle-tested across these failure modes.

Last-Event-ID for exactly-once resumption sounds clean in theory but has inconsistent behavior in practice — doesn't survive tab restarts, behaves differently across browsers when the connection was interrupted by a hard network failure versus a clean close, and HTTP/2's multiplexing means EventSource connections share fate with other requests on the same session.

The HTTP/2 multiplexing advantage for SSE cuts both ways. HTTP/2 connections are more aggressively terminated on mobile networks than HTTP/1.1 because they carry more state. When that single HTTP/2 connection drops, all your SSE streams go down together — and the reconnection thundering herd is worse than WebSocket's per-connection reconnect.

The claim that SSE is 'ready for production at scale' skips past the biggest failure mode I've seen: EventSource silently stops firing onerror in certain browser states (background tabs, low-memory mobile Safari, aggressive Chromium throttling). WebSocket's onclose/onerror are more consistently reliable across browser implementations because the reconnection logic is in application code that you control, not in a browser API that may or may not fire.

new_insights: The real divide isn't WebSocket vs SSE — it's controlled-infrastructure vs. adversarial-network. On infrastructure you own and tune, SSE wins on simplicity. On networks you don't control (mobile, public wifi, emerging markets), both transports need serious reconnection investment, and WebSocket's battle-tested patterns are more mature.

The HTTP/2 + SSE story is more fragile than I'd appreciated. HTTP/2 multiplexing means all SSE connections share fate on a single transport, and when that drops, recovery is more complex than independent WebSocket reconnections.

The 'SSE has built-in reconnection' argument is partly a trap. Native EventSource reconnection is minimal and browser-dependent. Production SSE implementations still need a wrapper layer for exponential backoff, connection health monitoring, and fallback transport — same as WebSocket. The difference is documentation quality, not complexity.

Neither transport solves the captive portal problem. Both silently fail on WiFi networks that require browser-based authentication. The real engineering challenge is detection and graceful degradation — something most teams discover post-ship, regardless of transport choice.
